title: The woman who invented George Sand
gist: A review of Fiona Sampson's 'Becoming George' argues that George Sand - hugely influential on the English novel yet forgotten outside France - was a deliberate act of self-invention, and that the biography restores her despite an uneven, occasionally dismissive tone.

insights.claim: Much of the English novel - Middlemarch, Wuthering Heights, Tess of the d'Urbervilles - owes a large debt to the 70-plus works of George Sand, who has nonetheless been largely forgotten outside France.
insights.so_what: Reputation-vs-influence gap: an under-recognized foundational figure ripe for reappraisal.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis

insights.claim: Fiona Sampson's 'Becoming George' (Doubleday, GBP 22) is timed to the 150th anniversary of Sand's death and is a serious attempt to restore her to English-language readers.
insights.so_what: Anniversary-driven cultural revival of a neglected canonical writer.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis

insights.claim: Sand's 1832 novel 'Indiana', published under the name G. Sand, framed marriage as domestic captivity and articulated a desire for autonomy, completing her transformation from Aurore Dupin into the 'George Sand' persona.
insights.so_what: The pseudonymous persona was itself the creative act that made her career.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis

insights.claim: Sampson argues that even Sand's letters and autobiographical writing should be read as narrative, meaning Sand may be more fully present in her fiction than in her nonfiction.
insights.so_what: A caution about treating self-authored records as transparent fact.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis

insights.claim: The biography's early chapters lean heavily on Sand's own 'Histoire de ma vie' and read as retelling rather than reinterpretation, and Sampson's tone occasionally turns dismissive ('hormonal', 'little dumpling').
insights.so_what: A flagged weakness for readers weighing the book's originality.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis

insights.claim: The reviewer contends that Sand's refusal to conform and her impulsivity are precisely what make her compelling, so Sampson's mix of admiration and admonishment jars.
insights.so_what: Interpretive tension in how transgressive women writers are narrated.
insights.speaker: Katherine Pangonis
